title: Statistical Inference and Hypothesis Testing
description: Master the fundamentals of hypothesis testing, p-values, and statistical decision-making through an adaptive learning pathway.

nodes.title: Introduction to Hypothesis Testing
nodes.content: Hypothesis testing is a structured method for making decisions about populations based on sample data. You'll start by formulating two competing hypotheses: the null hypothesis (H₀), which represents no effect or the status quo, and the alternative hypothesis (H₁), which represents what you're trying to demonstrate. For example, a pharmaceutical company might test H₀: the new drug has no effect versus H₁: the new drug improves outcomes. This process allows us to quantify uncertainty and control the probability of making incorrect conclusions.

nodes.title: Assessment 1: Understanding Hypotheses
nodes.content: Test your understanding of null and alternative hypotheses, and the structure of hypothesis testing.

questions.question: A company wants to test whether their new website design increases user engagement. Which statement correctly describes the null hypothesis?
options: The new design increases engagement
The new design has no effect on engagement
The new design decreases engagement
We cannot determine the null hypothesis without more data
feedback: Incorrect. The null hypothesis assumes no effect or change.
Correct! The null hypothesis represents the status quo—no effect.
Incorrect. This would be an alternative hypothesis for a one-tailed test in the negative direction.
Incorrect. We can always formulate H₀ and H₁ from the research question.
questions.explanation: The null hypothesis always states that there is no effect or difference. In this case, H₀: the design has no effect, and H₁: the design increases engagement.

questions.question: When conducting a hypothesis test, the alternative hypothesis is:
options: Always true
What you're trying to find evidence for
The same as the null hypothesis
Proven when p > 0.05
feedback: Incorrect. The alternative hypothesis is what we hope to support with evidence.
Correct! The alternative hypothesis represents the claim we're testing for.
Incorrect. H₀ and H₁ are mutually exclusive and exhaustive.
Incorrect. We reject H₀ (not prove H₁) when p < α.
questions.explanation: The alternative hypothesis is the research hypothesis—what you expect to be true. We gather evidence to test against H₀.

questions.question: Which scenario best describes a two-tailed hypothesis test?
options: Testing if a new fertilizer increases crop yield (one direction)
Testing if a treatment differs from the control in either direction
Testing only positive effects
Testing only negative effects
feedback: Incorrect. This is a one-tailed test (directional).
Correct! Two-tailed tests check for differences in both directions.
Incorrect. That would be a one-tailed test.
Incorrect. That would also be one-tailed.
questions.explanation: A two-tailed test has H₁: parameter ≠ value, testing for difference in either direction. One-tailed tests specify direction: > or <.

nodes.title: Practice: Hypothesis Formulation
nodes.content: Practice formulating hypothesis pairs for different research scenarios. Remember: the null hypothesis typically assumes no effect, difference, or relationship, while the alternative hypothesis is directional (one-tailed) or non-directional (two-tailed). Focus on clearly stating what you expect and what the null position would be.

questions.question: A researcher suspects that caffeine improves reaction time. What would be an appropriate alternative hypothesis?
options: H₁: Caffeine has no effect on reaction time
H₁: Caffeine improves reaction time
H₁: Caffeine decreases reaction time
H₁: Reaction time depends on caffeine type
feedback: Incorrect. This is the null hypothesis.
Correct! This matches the researcher's suspicion.
Incorrect. The researcher suspects improvement, not worsening.
Incorrect. This goes beyond the original hypothesis.
questions.explanation: Since the researcher suspects caffeine improves reaction time, H₁ should reflect this directional expectation. H₀ would be: caffeine has no effect on reaction time.

questions.question: For a clinical trial testing a new pain medication, which represents H₀?
options: The medication is more effective than placebo
The medication has no effect compared to placebo
Patients prefer the medication
The medication is dangerous
feedback: Incorrect. This is what we might hope H₁ would support.
Correct! The null hypothesis assumes no benefit.
Incorrect. This is about preference, not the medication's effect.
Incorrect. We would test safety separately.
questions.explanation: In clinical trials, H₀ assumes the new treatment has no advantage over existing treatment or placebo. We seek evidence to reject this.

questions.question: A school wants to test if a new tutoring program changes student math scores. Which is the correct H₀?
options: H₀: The program improves scores
H₀: The program has no effect on scores
H₀: All students benefit equally
H₀: The program hurts some students
feedback: Incorrect. This would be H₁.
Correct! H₀ assumes no change from the program.
Incorrect. H₀ doesn't specify distribution of effects.
Incorrect. H₀ is about overall effect, not individual harm.
questions.explanation: The null hypothesis always represents the position of no effect or no difference. Only if we find strong evidence do we reject H₀ in favor of H₁.

nodes.title: P-Values, Significance Levels, and Decision Rules
nodes.content: The p-value is the probability of observing your data (or more extreme data) if the null hypothesis were true. It quantifies how unusual your sample results are under H₀. If the p-value is less than your predetermined significance level (α, typically 0.05), you reject the null hypothesis—this means your sample provides sufficient evidence against H₀. It's crucial to understand that rejecting H₀ doesn't prove H₁ is true; it simply means the data are unlikely under H₀. Common significance levels are α = 0.05 (5%), 0.01 (1%), and 0.10 (10%), depending on the consequences of a Type I error (false positive). Type I error (α) is rejecting a true H₀, while Type II error (β) is failing to reject a false H₀.

nodes.title: Assessment 2: P-Values and Significance
nodes.content: Evaluate your understanding of p-values, significance levels, and decision rules in hypothesis testing.

questions.question: A hypothesis test yields a p-value of 0.03. If α = 0.05, what should you conclude?
options: Fail to reject H₀ because p > α
Reject H₀ because p < α
Accept H₀ as definitely true
The result is inconclusive
feedback: Incorrect. Since p = 0.03 is less than 0.05, we reject H₀.
Correct! When p < α, we reject H₀ at the chosen significance level.
Incorrect. Rejecting H₀ is not the same as accepting H₀.
Incorrect. With α = 0.05 and p = 0.03, the result is clear.
questions.explanation: The decision rule is simple: if p ≤ α, reject H₀; if p > α, fail to reject H₀. Here, 0.03 < 0.05, so we reject H₀.

questions.question: What does a p-value of 0.20 in a study mean?
options: There's a 20% chance H₀ is true
There's a 20% probability your sample results (or more extreme) would occur if H₀ were true
The alternative hypothesis is true with 80% probability
You should reject H₀ at α = 0.05
feedback: Incorrect. The p-value is not the probability that H₀ is true.
Correct! The p-value is computed under the assumption that H₀ is true.
Incorrect. The p-value doesn't directly tell us the probability H₁ is true.
Incorrect. If p = 0.20 > α = 0.05, we fail to reject H₀.
questions.explanation: The p-value is defined as P(data|H₀). It's the probability of observing these results under H₀, not the probability that H₀ is true.

questions.question: Which scenario represents a Type I error?
options: Rejecting H₀ when it's actually true
Failing to reject H₀ when it's actually false
Correctly rejecting a false H₀
Correctly failing to reject a true H₀
feedback: Correct! Type I error is a false positive—rejecting H₀ when it's true.
Incorrect. This is a Type II error (false negative).
Incorrect. This is a correct decision.
Incorrect. This is also a correct decision.
questions.explanation: Type I error (α) = rejecting H₀ when H₀ is true (false alarm). Type II error (β) = failing to reject H₀ when H₀ is false (missed detection).

nodes.title: Practice: Interpreting P-Values and Making Decisions
nodes.content: Apply your understanding of p-values and decision rules to realistic scenarios. Remember: the significance level α is your predetermined threshold, and comparing p to α determines your decision. Always compare the p-value to your chosen significance level—that's the core decision rule.

questions.question: In a quality control test, p-value = 0.08 and α = 0.05. What decision do you make?
options: Reject H₀
Fail to reject H₀
Accept H₀ as proven true
Increase sample size before deciding
feedback: Incorrect. Since p = 0.08 > α = 0.05, we don't have sufficient evidence.
Correct! When p > α, we fail to reject H₀ at this significance level.
Incorrect. Failing to reject H₀ is not proof that H₀ is true.
Incorrect. We already have a decision rule with our α level.
questions.explanation: The decision rule is universal: p ≤ α means reject H₀; p > α means fail to reject H₀. Here, 0.08 > 0.05, so we fail to reject H₀.

questions.question: If a researcher uses α = 0.01 (stricter than typical 0.05), what are they trying to minimize?
options: Type I errors (false positives)
Type II errors (false negatives)
Sample size
The importance of the results
feedback: Correct! A lower α means fewer Type I errors but more Type II errors.
Incorrect. A stricter α (lower) actually increases Type II error risk.
Incorrect. Sample size is independent of the significance level choice.
Incorrect. Significance level doesn't affect result importance.
questions.explanation: Lowering α from 0.05 to 0.01 makes it harder to reject H₀, reducing false positives but increasing false negatives (Type II errors).

questions.question: A study compares two treatments. p-value = 0.0001 and α = 0.05. What's the most accurate statement?
options: We definitely proved H₁ is true
We reject H₀; the data provide strong evidence against H₀
The result is practically significant
The treatment difference is large
feedback: Incorrect. Rejecting H₀ doesn't prove H₁; it shows evidence against H₀.
Correct! A very small p-value provides strong evidence against H₀.
Incorrect. Statistical significance doesn't guarantee practical significance.
Incorrect. A small p-value doesn't tell us effect size.
questions.explanation: A very small p-value (0.0001) indicates the observed data are extremely unlikely under H₀, providing strong evidence against it. But significance ≠ practical importance.

nodes.title: Effect Size and Statistical Power
nodes.content: Statistical significance (small p-value) doesn't always mean practical significance—this is where effect size comes in. Effect size quantifies the magnitude of the difference or relationship (e.g., Cohen's d for comparing means: small d ≈ 0.2, medium d ≈ 0.5, large d ≈ 0.8). A large sample can produce statistically significant results from tiny, meaningless effects. Conversely, statistical power (typically 0.80 or 80%) is the probability of detecting a real effect when it exists. Power depends on effect size, sample size, and significance level. Assumptions matter too: many tests require normally distributed data, independence of observations, or equal variances. Always check these before interpreting results. A complete statistical analysis considers significance, effect size, power, and assumptions together.

nodes.title: Assessment 3: Comprehensive Understanding
nodes.content: Demonstrate mastery of hypothesis testing by integrating significance, effect size, power, and assumptions.

questions.question: A study finds p = 0.02 with Cohen's d = 0.10. What is the most appropriate interpretation?
options: The result is statistically significant and practically important
The result is statistically significant but has a small practical effect size
The result is not statistically significant
We need a larger sample size to determine significance
feedback: Incorrect. d = 0.10 is considered a small effect, not practically important.
Correct! p = 0.02 < 0.05 shows significance, but d = 0.10 is a small effect.
Incorrect. p = 0.02 < α = 0.05, so the result is statistically significant.
Incorrect. We already have clear significance; effect size is the concern here.
questions.explanation: Statistical significance and practical significance are different. A large sample can detect tiny effects. Here, significant but small effect means limited practical utility.

questions.question: Statistical power of 0.90 means:
options: There's a 90% probability that H₀ is false
There's a 90% probability of detecting a true effect if it exists
The p-value will definitely be less than 0.10
The study result is 90% likely to be true
feedback: Incorrect. Power doesn't tell us if H₀ is false in reality.
Correct! Power is the probability of rejecting H₀ when H₀ is false.
Incorrect. Power and p-values are related but distinct concepts.
Incorrect. Power is a frequentist probability, not about this result.
questions.explanation: Power = P(reject H₀ | H₀ is false). A power of 0.90 means 90% chance of detecting a real effect if it truly exists.

questions.question: Which is most important when checking test assumptions?
options: Using the test anyway if the sample is large enough
Verifying that conditions are met before interpreting results
Assuming assumptions are always satisfied
Choosing a significance level based on the data
feedback: Incorrect. Large samples don't excuse assumption violations.
Correct! Always verify assumptions before trusting your test results.
Incorrect. We must check assumptions for each analysis.
Incorrect. α must be set before collecting data (pre-registered).
questions.explanation: Violating test assumptions (normality, independence, equal variance) can invalidate your results. Always check assumptions before interpreting conclusions.

nodes.title: Practice: Effect Size and Power in Context
nodes.content: Integrate your understanding of significance, effect size, and power. Consider both statistical and practical significance when evaluating research conclusions. Think critically about what results mean in real-world contexts.

questions.question: A drug trial finds a statistically significant reduction in symptoms (p = 0.04) but effect size d = 0.15. What should clinicians consider?
options: The drug should definitely be prescribed to all patients
The statistical significance doesn't guarantee clinical meaningfulness; the small effect size may not justify treatment
The results are meaningless because p > 0.05
Effect size doesn't matter if p < 0.05
feedback: Incorrect. Statistical significance alone doesn't justify clinical use.
Correct! Practical significance requires both p < α AND meaningful effect size.
Incorrect. p = 0.04 < 0.05, so the result is statistically significant.
Incorrect. Effect size is crucial; statistical significance can be trivial.
questions.explanation: Medical decisions require both statistical significance and practical importance. A tiny effect (d = 0.15) may not warrant changing clinical practice.

questions.question: To increase statistical power, which approach is most effective?
options: Lower the significance level (α)
Increase sample size
Use a weaker alternative hypothesis
Ignore effect size
feedback: Incorrect. Lowering α actually decreases power.
Correct! Larger samples improve ability to detect true effects.
Incorrect. The alternative hypothesis is fixed by the research question.
Incorrect. Effect size is one of the main determinants of power.
questions.explanation: The main factors affecting power are: effect size (bigger = higher power), sample size (larger = higher power), and significance level (lower α = lower power).

questions.question: A researcher reports: 'We found no significant effect (p = 0.12) but power was only 0.60.' What does this mean?
options: The null hypothesis is definitely true
A real effect probably doesn't exist
There was only 60% chance to detect a real effect; low power makes null results inconclusive
The study was a complete failure
feedback: Incorrect. Failing to reject H₀ doesn't prove it's true.
Incorrect. Low power makes it hard to conclude anything from null results.
Correct! Low power means weak ability to detect effects; the study is inconclusive.
Incorrect. Low power studies can still be informative if properly interpreted.
questions.explanation: When power is low (< 0.80), a null result is inconclusive. The study may have missed a real effect. High power is needed for both positive and negative results.

nodes.title: Mastery Achieved: Statistical Inference and Hypothesis Testing
nodes.content: Congratulations! You've mastered the fundamentals of hypothesis testing and statistical inference. You now understand how to formulate hypotheses, interpret p-values, control error rates, consider effect sizes, and design powerful studies. These concepts are essential for evaluating research claims, conducting experiments, and making evidence-based decisions. Continue applying these principles in your statistical work, remembering that statistical significance and practical significance are different—always consider both when interpreting results. You're ready to advance to specialized topics like specific hypothesis tests, Bayesian approaches, or multivariate inference.
